<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: system-boundaries-and-authority
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/system-boundaries-and-authority.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/system-boundaries-and-authority.qmd@753d3404f
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# System Boundaries and Authority

The stack frame and the efficiency claim now need the guardrail that makes
both ideas usable: the system must know the difference
between what a component can do and what it may do.

That distinction is not cosmetic. A planner can describe a correct action. A
model can produce a useful answer. A memory system can surface relevant
context. A specialist can generate a patch. None of those facts should
automatically authorize external action. Capability answers one question.
Authority answers another.

In a governed stack, refusal is not a breakdown in intelligence. It is one of
the ways intelligence stays inside its contract. The system must be able to
say: the plan is coherent, the source is available, the model is capable, and
the action is still not authorized.

By the end, "authority" should feel less like an abstract safety word and more
like a concrete transition record: who is asking, what boundary they want to
cross, which grant they have, which ceiling binds them, what happens if the
grant is missing, and what receipt remains after the decision.

## Problem

The stack needs a formal vocabulary for boundaries before it can make serious
claims about planning, memory, tools, deployment, or self-improvement.

Efficiency routes cognition by cost and quality. That only works if route
selection remains separate from permission to act. A planner may know how to
decompose a task without being allowed to touch production. A memory layer may
read a source without being allowed to reveal it. A model may understand a
private instruction without being allowed to disclose it. A runtime adapter
may know the shell command that would finish the job without being allowed to
run it.

Those separations need records. Authority cannot be a mood, trust score, role
label, or reputation assigned to a model. It has to be a typed, bounded
capability attached to a principal, layer, tool, field, artifact, route, or
handoff. It has to expire, be revocable, leave audit evidence, and fail closed
when the grant is absent.

The source queue converges on that pressure from different
directions. VIEA needs authority boundaries so intent-to-execution does not
become intent-to-side-effect. SCF needs them so a replacement implementation
does not inherit powers simply by occupying a stable capability field. Talos
needs them so typed jobs, controlled adapters, blind secret handles, and
approval gates stay inside a control plane. Ladon/Manhattan and GenesisCode
make the same point for secrets, effects, patches, executors, and protocol
seals. MoECOT remains implementation-reference context rather than reproduced
runtime evidence.

The core danger is permission collapse. Read access becomes write access.
Planning becomes execution. A source summary becomes authorization. A
benchmark result becomes promotion authority. A generated patch becomes
accepted code. A capable specialist receives the task because it can perform
the work, not because the caller may authorize the work.

When that collapse happens, the architecture has lost the boundary that lets
it be governed.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Informal boundaries are too weak for systems that can plan, remember, call
tools, write artifacts, and change themselves.

Prompt instructions and role labels can help a human understand intent, but
they do not define a real permission system. "You are an assistant" does not
say whether the system may read a private file, call a shell, modify a branch,
push a commit, spend money, export a source, update an evaluator, or approve
its own replacement. Tool wrappers are closer to enforcement, but wrappers
still need typed authority. Otherwise a list of available functions becomes a
list of tempting side effects.

The external sources are useful for locating the territory, not for proving
this design. NIST risk-management and frontier-governance work provide
vocabulary for lifecycle duties, assessment, oversight, and roles. Formal work
on power-seeking pressure warns that capable agents can prefer options that
preserve or expand their influence under broad objectives. Those sources help
explain why authority boundaries matter. They do not show that this repository
has deployed enforcement.

The security lineage is more specific. Classic protection principles give the
authority layer least-privilege and complete-mediation language.
Capability-system work treats some references as carrying authority rather
than only naming an object. Hardy's confused-deputy problem names the failure
that keeps returning here: a lower-authority requester induces a higher-authority tool to
act under the tool's broader power. The ASI Stack does not claim to implement
object-capability security, but it uses that lineage to explain why caller
ceilings, delegation chains, expiry, and effect receipts have to travel with
the handoff.

The failure mode becomes sharper under self-improvement. A replacement
implementation may be better at the task while accidentally receiving the old
implementation's handles. A learned route may be cheaper while granting itself
approval shortcuts. A tool call may run under the user's broad session power
instead of the requesting layer's narrow grant. A memory system may treat a
source it can see as a source it can reveal.

Authority records are the counterweight. They make the system ask "may this
principal cross this boundary under this grant?" before it asks "can some
component perform the operation?"

## Core Claim

Authority should be modeled as a typed, bounded capability attached to layers,
fields, tools, artifacts, and principals. The live book keeps this claim at
`argument` support.

That boundary matters because the rest of the stack depends on it. Planning is
not execution. Reading is not disclosure. Transforming an artifact is not
approving it. Routing to a specialist is not granting that specialist every
handle in the session. Replacing an implementation is not a reason to widen
the field's authority ceiling.

The source mappings in Appendix C support the architectural direction, and
the local repository has useful narrow artifacts: an authority-transition
schema, synthetic fixtures, a validation harness, and finite Lean predicates.
Those artifacts do not prove deployed authorization enforcement, runtime
adapter safety, revocation propagation, live confused-deputy resistance, or
tool-wrapper security. The claim strengthens only when those surfaces exist
and pass review.

## Mechanism

Authority is the stack's answer to the question "who may cause an effect?"

The mechanism is an Authority Transition Record. It sits at the boundary where
a principal, layer, field, tool, artifact, or route asks to cross from one
state into another. Before the effect happens, the record names the requester,
the requested operation, the boundary being crossed, the permission class, the
caller ceiling, the target's required authority, the grant, the revocation or
expiry state, the decision, and the evidence that remains afterward.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Principal / layer"] --> B["Requested operation"]
  B --> C["Authority ceiling"]
  B --> D["Required grant"]
  C --> E{"Within ceiling?"}
  D --> F{"Grant present<br/>and unrevoked?"}
  E -- "no" --> G["Deny + audit"]
  F -- "no" --> G
  E -- "yes" --> H["Authorized handoff"]
  F -- "yes" --> H
  H --> I["Executor / tool / field"]
  I --> J["Effect receipt"]
  J --> K["Evidence ledger"]
```

Read the diagram as a permission gate, not a capability test. The request
crosses the boundary only when both the ceiling and grant checks pass. A
failure is not ignored. It becomes a denial or escalation record that the
evidence ledger can inspect later.

This is what keeps authority out of semantic similarity. A model may infer
that an action would help. That inference is not a grant. A retrieved document
may contain instructions. That text is not a grant. A specialist may be the
highest-quality route. Quality is not a grant. The grant is a record issued by
governance or by an authorized caller under a bounded delegation rule.

Authority also has a lifecycle. A grant is not a permanent property of a
model, layer, or user session. It is scoped to a principal, operation, target,
time or epoch, approval state, and evidence obligation.

```{mermaid}
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> Requested
  Requested --> Denied: over ceiling or missing grant
  Requested --> Granted: scoped approval
  Granted --> Delegated: bounded handoff
  Delegated --> Used: adapter/effect call
  Used --> Receipted: effect receipt
  Granted --> Revoked: policy or expiry
  Delegated --> Revoked: policy or expiry
  Revoked --> Denied: later use attempt
  Receipted --> [*]
  Denied --> [*]
```

The important word in that diagram is "bounded." Delegation can narrow
authority. It should not silently widen it. A layer may pass a task to a tool
or specialist only within the ceiling it actually holds, and later use must
fail when the grant expires or is revoked.

## Interfaces

Authority changes travel through an Authority Transition Record. Governance
issues ceilings and grants. Planning and routing may request handoffs, but
they do not thereby authorize them. Execution checks permissions before side
effects. Evidence records denials, confused-deputy attempts, revocations, and
authorized effects.

At minimum, the record needs to say:

- who or what is asking;
- which layer or principal owns the request;
- which boundary is being crossed;
- what operation is requested;
- which permission class applies;
- what the caller's ceiling is;
- what authority the target requires;
- which grant or delegation chain is active;
- whether the grant has expired or been revoked;
- whether the decision is allow, deny, or escalate;
- what denial reason or effect receipt was produced;
- which audit references and non-claims travel with the decision.

The record does not prove enforcement by itself. It shows where enforcement
would be tested. Did the requested operation match the permission class? Did
delegation narrow rather than widen the caller's ceiling? Did revocation block
later use? Did execution produce a receipt? Did denial leave a reason that
future planning cannot reinterpret as missing context?

### Permission Classes

The record has to name the kind of permission being requested, because the
same information can be safe in one class and unsafe in another.

Read permission lets a layer inspect material without publishing it, mutating
it, or acting on it. Transform permission lets a layer derive a new artifact,
but it carries provenance and taint obligations. Disclosure permission asks
whether the output may be shown, exported, or transmitted to the intended
audience. Write permission changes a file, ledger, memory cell, branch, or
other durable target and therefore needs target-scoped authority plus audit.
Execute permission crosses into a tool, runtime, service, or adapter and
should leave an effect receipt. Approval permission is still more constrained:
it authorizes a promotion or high-impact action and cannot be self-issued by
the actor that benefits from it.

These categories are deliberately ordinary because most authority failures are
ordinary. A model reads something and treats the text as approval. A planner
turns a draft into an action. A low-authority request borrows a high-authority
tool. A replacement implementation inherits a handle that was qualified for an
older context. The point of the taxonomy is to make those mistakes visible
before they become side effects.

## Invariants and Failure Modes

The authority layer has a small set of invariants that later chapters keep
reusing.

Authority never expands silently. Read permission is not write permission.
Tool execution requires an explicit grant. Delegated authority can narrow but
not silently widen the caller's ceiling. Approval authority is separate from
execution authority.

Those invariants turn common failures into inspectable records:

- authority creep;
- confused-deputy tool calls;
- memory access treated as action approval;
- stale grants used after expiry or revocation;
- replacement implementations inheriting handles qualified for an older
  implementation.

The confused-deputy pattern is the central test. A low-authority layer asks a
higher-authority tool to do something that the layer itself could not do. If
the tool runs under its own raw capability rather than the requester's ceiling,
the boundary has failed. The fix is to bind the operation to the requester's
authority, not merely to the tool's power.

In practice, that means a tool call should not inherit authority simply
because the tool can perform the operation. The request has to carry its own
ceiling, grant, delegation chain, expiry state, and receipt obligation. If
those fields are missing, denial is the correct result even when the tool
would succeed technically.

Memory creates the parallel version of the same failure. Seeing a secret,
policy, instruction, or source is mistaken for permission to disclose or act
on it. The stack has to keep those operations separate even when the text
itself is fluent, relevant, or urgent.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest useful implementation is not a universal permission kernel. It is
an `authority_transition_record` schema plus fixtures and checks for the
smallest boundaries the stack already needs.

The fixture set should include an allowed handoff, an over-ceiling denial, an
approval escalation, a missing-receipt failure, a permission-collapse failure,
and a confused-deputy failure. The first negative fixture is as important as
the allowed one. A route that has useful context but lacks authority should
produce a denial record before any downstream layer can treat the handoff as
executable.

The repository already has narrow pieces of that surface. Protocol validation
checks authority records. `AsiStackProofs.Authority` contains finite-record
predicates about ceiling preservation and missing-grant denial. The authority
transition harness exercises synthetic allow, denial, escalation,
missing-receipt, permission-collapse, and confused-deputy cases.

Those are useful foundations, not deployed enforcement. They do not show a
live tool wrapper resisting a confused-deputy attempt, a runtime adapter
propagating revocation, or a production system denying stale authority. The
support state stays `argument`.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature authority layer is a type system for effects.

Every model, tool, memory cell, field, artifact, human, project, and runtime
route would carry explicit capability bounds. The system would distinguish
what a component can do from what it may do at every handoff. Authority would
be typed, bounded, revocable, durable, and attached to concrete principals,
layers, fields, tools, artifacts, and routes.

In that mature architecture, denial receipts matter as much as grants. Missing
authority leaves an inspectable trail. Authority creep becomes a failed
transition. Memory access treated as action approval becomes a denied handoff.
Execution without a grant becomes an audit event. Replacement that widens a
field's authority ceiling becomes a blocked qualification. Self-improvement
cannot quietly turn better performance into broader permission.

This remains a target architecture. The local schemas, fixtures, harnesses,
and Lean predicates exercise narrow record-level behavior. They do not prove
runtime permission enforcement, revocation propagation, tool-call security, or
deployed governance.

## Summary

Governance becomes operational only when authority is a transition system
rather than a feeling. A layer may propose, read, transform, disclose, write,
execute, approve, or deploy only when the matching grant exists and remains
inside the active ceiling. Everything else is a denial, escalation, missing
receipt, or missing-authority record.

That boundary keeps the efficient stack from becoming a permission leak.
Routing can choose a worker. Planning can request a handoff. Memory can expose
context. GenesisCode-like systems can propose effects. But authority moves
only through typed transitions that can be audited, revoked, tested, and later
formalized.

Without those transitions, the rest of the architecture gets deceptively easy.
Context becomes permission. Capability becomes authority. Benchmarks become
promotion. Tool access becomes deployment. The stack needs the authority layer
because it must remain able to stop an action even when it knows how to do it.

## Handoff

Once authority is explicit, the architecture needs a map of what breaks when
authority is missing, vague, or exceeded. Failure Modes of Ungoverned
Intelligence turns the boundary rules into a failure radar: authority creep,
context pollution, evaluator capture, evidence inflation, and unbounded
execution become detectable architectural problems rather than vague safety
concerns. The sequence moves from the positive type system to the negative map
that every later layer must answer.
